seo.meta_description: Meet Franciele Sofiati, a biomédica in Londrina whose approach to skin, laser and advanced aesthetic care begins with listening, assessment and honest guidance.

blocks.text: Meet the person behind calm, consultation-led aesthetic care in Londrina.
Aesthetic concerns are visible, but the reasons behind them are often more complex. Skin, hair, expression or body changes can affect how someone feels in photographs, at work or in the mirror.
I chose this work because it allows me to bring together scientific knowledge, careful observation and human connection.
✦  Scientific precision
Understanding how technologies, products and treatment depth interact with skin and tissue.
◇  Individual response
Recognising that no two routines, concerns or recoveries are exactly the same.
○  Subtle change
Planning improvement without asking every person to pursue the same result.
◎  Continuity
Building trust through preparation, aftercare and thoughtful review.
I do not believe aesthetic care should make everyone look the same.
There is no single face, skin texture, expression or proportion that every person should be encouraged to pursue. What feels harmonious for one person may feel unnecessary or unnatural for another.
✓  I look for

items.text: Balance without uniformity
Change without loss of expression
Improvement without exaggerated promises
Technology used for a clear reason
items.raw_text: • Technology used for a clear reason

blocks.text: ◇  I step away from

items.text: Trend-led standardisation
Pressure to keep changing
Treating natural variation as failure
Intervention for intervention’s sake

blocks.text: “The most elegant result is often the one that still feels completely like you.”

paragraphs.text: “The most elegant result is often the one that still feels completely like you.”

blocks.title: Responsible answer
blocks.text: A recommendation can also be to prepare first, postpone, refer or avoid treatment.
✦  I listen first
Your experience and reason for seeking care come before the treatment name.
◇  I explain the reasoning
You should understand why an option is being considered and what it cannot guarantee.
○  I respect your limits
Your questions, preferences and boundaries belong inside the clinical conversation.
◎  I discuss recovery honestly
Preparation, temporary reactions and aftercare are considered before treatment begins.
↗  I leave room to reconsider
You never need to decide simply because you attended a consultation.
⌁  I adapt the plan
Timing, treatment intervals and next steps can change according to your response.
“Trust is built when the explanation is as careful as the procedure.”

paragraphs.text: “Trust is built when the explanation is as careful as the procedure.”

blocks.text: The clinic is designed to feel soft, welcoming and refined without losing clinical precision. A calm setting creates room to speak, ask and understand without feeling hurried.
✦  Privacy
Personal concerns are discussed with discretion.
◇  Calm
There is time to understand rather than react.
○  Clarity
Information is communicated without unnecessary complexity.
◎  Warmth
Professional care can still feel genuinely human.
↗  Attention
Small details help the experience feel considered.
⌁  Choice
You remain free to pause, ask or decide later.
“Softness belongs in the environment. Precision belongs in the decision.”

paragraphs.text: “Softness belongs in the environment. Precision belongs in the decision.”

blocks.text: Advanced aesthetic technologies can offer valuable possibilities, but the device itself is never the entire treatment. The quality of the decision depends on the indication, treatment settings, preparation, technique and follow-up.
✦  Skin care and renewal
Cleansing, controlled exfoliation, chemical renewal and pigmentation-focused planning.
◇  Laser and light
Selected approaches for hair reduction, pigmentation, vessels, texture, scars and resurfacing.
○  Firmness and support
Focused ultrasound, radiofrequency and other selected energy-based protocols.
◎  Expression and balance
Botulinum toxin planning for selected patterns of muscular movement.
↗  Scalp care
Assessment-led microinfusion and intradermal treatment pathways.
⌁  Superficial microvessels
Selected injectable treatment following appropriate vascular assessment.

seo.meta_description: Learn how the Franciele Sofiati website aims to support accessible browsing, readable content, keyboard use and clear contact options.
seo.search_themes: website accessibility, accessible aesthetic clinic website, Franciele Sofiati accessibility

blocks.text: Use the site in the way that works best for you.

items.text: Logical heading order and page structure;
Readable text with sufficient contrast;
Keyboard navigation for interactive elements;
Visible focus states for links, buttons and form fields;
Alternative text for meaningful images;
Labels and instructions for forms;
Content that remains understandable when enlarged;
Reduced reliance on colour alone to communicate meaning.

blocks.text: ✓  Continuous improvement
The website may evolve as pages, tools and technologies change. Accessibility checks should continue whenever new content or components are introduced.
✦  Zoom
Use your browser controls to enlarge text and page content.
◇  Keyboard
Move through links and form fields using Tab and Shift + Tab.
○  Screen readers
Headings and labels are intended to support assistive navigation.
◎  Language
Use the English or Portuguese version according to your preference.
↗  Motion
Animations should respect reduced-motion preferences where supported.
⌁  Contact alternatives
WhatsApp and email remain available when a form is difficult to use.
Contact and consultation forms should identify every field, explain why information is requested and communicate errors in text—not only through colour.

items.text: Required fields are marked clearly;
Instructions appear near the relevant field;
Error messages explain what needs to be corrected;
Success messages confirm when a submission has been received;
Time limits should not prevent a person from completing a form.

blocks.text: Meaningful clinical and editorial images should include concise alternative text. Decorative imagery may be hidden from assistive technology when it does not add information.
Videos should be supported by captions or transcripts whenever video content is published. Important instructions should also appear in text.
Aesthetic and skin-care terminology can be unfamiliar. I aim to explain important concepts in direct language while preserving clinical accuracy.
✓  Helpful content

items.text: Short paragraphs and descriptive headings
Treatment purpose and limitations explained together
items.raw_text: • Treatment purpose and limitations explained together

items.text: Clear routes into related pages
Plain-language form guidance

blocks.text: ◇  What we try to avoid

items.text: Instructions hidden in images
Links labelled only “click here”
Dense blocks without structure
Colour as the only signal

blocks.text: Some third-party services, embedded tools or external platforms may not offer the same level of accessibility as the main website. Their behaviour can also change independently.

blocks.text: ✦  Health and medication
Relevant conditions, allergies, medication, supplements, pregnancy, breastfeeding and recent illness may affect planning.
◇  Previous treatments
Lasers, peels, injectables, surgery, threads, tattoos and reactions can remain relevant.
○  Current products
Retinoids, acids, acne products, depigmenting formulas and home devices may affect tolerance.
◎  Sun and heat exposure
Recent tanning, sunburn or planned outdoor activity can change treatment timing.
↗  Your calendar
Travel, work, exercise, celebrations and photographs help determine an appropriate recovery window.
⌁  Your expectations
We discuss what may improve, what may require several stages and what cannot be promised.
Preparing well is a quiet way of caring for the result and for yourself.
✦  Cleansing and gentle renewal
Deep cleansing, diamond peel, crystal peel and ultrasonic peel may require a simple, calming routine and temporary pause from irritating products.
◇  Chemical renewal
Retinoic, Jessner, TCA and depigmentation protocols vary in depth. Flaking, redness and product restrictions depend on the exact peel.
○  Laser and resurfacing
Hair reduction, pigment treatment and ablative CO₂ resurfacing have very different recovery needs.
◎  Firmness and energy-based care
Focused ultrasound, radiofrequency and plasma technologies differ in target, depth, sensation and aftercare.
↗  Injectables and microinfusion
Botulinum toxin, MMP and scalp injections require treatment-specific guidance on pressure, products and activity.
⌁  Superficial microvessels
PEIM aftercare depends on the vessels treated, sclerosant used and individual vascular assessment.
The instructions I provide after your appointment take priority over general website information.

items.text: Use only the cleansers, moisturisers and repair products recommended for the procedure;
Avoid repeated touching, rubbing, scratching, squeezing or pressing;
Allow flakes, peeling or small crusts to release naturally;
Follow the sun-protection plan discussed for your treatment;
Ask before restarting retinoids, acids, scrubs or depigmenting products;
Keep anything that contacts the treated area clean.

blocks.text: Gentle, consistent care can be more valuable than doing too much.
✓  Do

items.text: Follow your individual instructions
Keep the routine simple and consistent
items.raw_text: • Keep the routine simple and consistent

items.text: Respect the recovery window
Send clear photographs when requested
Ask before combining treatments
Contact me when uncertain

blocks.text: ◇  Avoid

items.text: Picking, scratching or peeling
Copying another patient’s routine
Adding strong active products early
Unprotected sun exposure
Judging every result immediately
Treating aftercare as optional

blocks.text: You deserve to feel supported, even after you leave the treatment room.
✦  Exercise
Intense activity may need to be postponed after selected injectables, vascular care or deeper resurfacing.
◇  Heat
Hot showers, saunas, steam and prolonged heat may be unsuitable during early recovery.
○  Swimming
Pools, hot tubs, lakes and the sea may need to wait while the barrier is disrupted.
◎  Makeup
It may be allowed quickly after some procedures and restricted after others.
↗  Shaving and waxing
Hair-removal methods may need to be adjusted around laser, peels or resurfacing.
⌁  Massage and pressure
Do not massage injectable or energy-treated areas unless specifically instructed.
✓  Individual guidance only
I do not impose every possible restriction after every treatment. I explain what applies to the procedure you actually received.
Temporary redness, warmth, tenderness, swelling, bruising, dryness or peeling may occur after some procedures. The expected response depends on the treatment and should be explained before you leave.

items.text: Contact me if pain is severe or increasing;
Contact me if redness, swelling or heat continues to spread;
Contact me for blistering, discharge, an unexpected wound or marked colour change;
Contact me if altered sensation persists or intensifies;
Contact me before resuming a product or activity when you are unsure.

blocks.text: !  Seek urgent medical care

Do not wait for a routine clinic reply for difficulty breathing, speaking or swallowing; rapid facial or throat swelling; fainting; severe generalised weakness; significant visual disturbance; chest pain; sudden shortness of breath; coughing blood; or another serious, rapidly developing reaction.
My approach connects consultation, preparation, treatment, aftercare and review. This continuity shows how you responded and whether the plan should continue, adjust, simplify or pause.

seo.title: Aesthetic Treatment FAQ | Franciele Sofiati
seo.meta_description: Clear answers about aesthetic consultations, skin and laser suitability, preparation, recovery, aftercare and results with Franciele Sofiati.
seo.search_themes: aesthetic FAQ Londrina, laser questions, skin treatment questions, consultation FAQ

blocks.text: Straightforward answers about consultations, suitability, treatment, recovery and results.
Do I need to know which treatment I want?
No. Explain what you have noticed and what you would like help understanding. Choosing an appropriate option is part of the consultation.
What happens during a consultation?
Franciele listens to your concern, asks about relevant health and treatment history, assesses the area when appropriate and explains possible next steps, including their limitations.
Does a consultation oblige me to proceed?
No. You can ask questions, take time to think, prepare first or decide not to have a procedure.
How are sensitive concerns handled?
You can speak in your own words and share only what is relevant. Franciele approaches personal concerns with discretion and respect.
How does Franciele decide what may be suitable?
She considers the concern, area, current condition, health context, medication, previous procedures, preferences, timing and the recovery involved.
Can Franciele advise me not to have a procedure?
Yes. She may recommend waiting, preparing the area, choosing another approach or not treating when a procedure appears unsuitable.
Will I be encouraged to combine treatments?
Only when there is a clear reason and the sequence, cumulative irritation and recovery are appropriate. More treatment is not assumed to be better.
Can the treatment plan change later?
Yes. New health information, changes in the skin, timing or your response may alter the recommended procedure, settings, interval or number of sessions.
What information should I provide?
Mention relevant health conditions, allergies, pregnancy or breastfeeding, medication and supplements, recent sun exposure, previous procedures and current products when asked.
Can medication affect treatment?
Yes. Some medication can affect healing, bleeding, sensitivity, pigmentation or suitability. Do not stop prescribed medication unless the responsible prescriber advises you to.
When might another professional need to assess the concern?
Symptoms suggesting a medical condition, uncertain diagnosis or a concern outside aesthetic scope may need evaluation by a doctor or another appropriately qualified professional.
What happens if my skin is not ready?
Franciele may simplify your routine, recommend preparation, allow the barrier to recover or postpone the appointment.
Why can similar skin concerns need different plans?
Redness, dark marks, breakouts and texture can have different causes and depths. Their appearance alone may not show which approach is appropriate.
How many skincare products do I need?
There is no ideal number. A useful routine has a clear purpose, supports the skin barrier and avoids ingredients the skin cannot currently tolerate.
Are acne marks and acne scars the same?
No. Flat colour changes differ from structural changes in the skin. Some people have both, and each may need a different approach.
Should skincare sting to be effective?
No. Persistent burning or worsening irritation can signal that the routine is too aggressive or that the skin needs assessment.
Do all lasers do the same thing?
No. Different systems target different structures, such as hair pigment, colour, vessels or water in the skin. Their uses and recovery are not interchangeable.
Is laser suitable for every person and concern?
No. Skin characteristics, tanning, medication, treatment history, the target and the available technology all affect suitability.
Is the most powerful setting the best one?
No. The setting should aim for a useful response while respecting the surrounding tissue and individual risk.
Why are several laser sessions sometimes needed?
Hair cycles, pigment behaviour and gradual tissue responses can require a series. The recommended number and interval depend on the treatment and your response.
Will I need to prepare?
Possibly. Instructions may involve sun exposure, skincare, hair removal methods, products or timing. They depend on the specific procedure.
Should I stop medication beforehand?
Never stop prescribed medication on your own. Tell Franciele what you take so any concern can be discussed with the responsible prescriber.
Why should I mention recent sun exposure?
Tanning or sunburn can affect how some treatments interact with the skin and may increase risk, so an appointment may need to be adjusted or postponed.
Should I mention travel or an important event?
Yes. Recovery and sun exposure can affect timing. Sharing important dates helps Franciele advise whether the proposed schedule is sensible.
How long will recovery take?
It varies with the procedure, treatment intensity, area and individual response. Franciele will explain the expected course for the option being considered.
What can aftercare involve?
It may include gentle cleansing, moisturising, sun protection, temporary product changes and limits on heat, exercise, pressure or picking. Follow the instructions for your procedure.
When should I contact Franciele after treatment?
Get in touch when recovery differs from what was explained, especially if symptoms are increasing, persistent or worrying.
What if symptoms seem urgent?
Routine clinic messages are not an emergency service. Severe or rapidly worsening symptoms need prompt assessment through the appropriate medical or emergency service.
Why do results vary?
The starting concern, skin condition, age, lifestyle, treatment choice, healing response, session timing and aftercare can all influence the outcome.
How many sessions will I need?
Some care is planned as one procedure; other concerns may need a series or maintenance. A precise number cannot always be known before seeing how you respond.
How does Franciele approach natural-looking results?
She considers proportion, expression and your preferences, and does not treat greater intensity or more procedures as automatically better.
How is progress reviewed?
Your response is compared with your own baseline, photographs where appropriate and the agreed aim. The plan can then continue, change or stop.
Where does Franciele practise?
Franciele provides care in Londrina, Paraná. The exact location and arrival details are confirmed when an appointment is arranged.
How should I contact the clinic?
Use WhatsApp at (43) 9 9104-3536, email suportesofiati@gmail.com or the contact form. A message is an enquiry until the clinic confirms an appointment.
How are expectations discussed?
Franciele explains the likely degree and timing of improvement, important limitations, possible maintenance and the fact that outcomes differ.
What guides Franciele’s recommendations?
Listening, suitability, clarity, restraint, individuality, safety, honesty, respect, continuity and professional responsibility.
You deserve answers that help you feel informed, confident and free to choose.

blocks.text: Understand what different technologies do and why assessment matters.
✦  Melanin
Pigment in hair and skin. It may be targeted in hair-reduction and selected pigmentary treatments.
◇  Haemoglobin
A component of blood targeted by selected technologies for superficial vessels or redness.
○  Water
A principal target in ablative CO₂ resurfacing, allowing controlled microscopic treatment.
◎  Tattoo pigment
Selected wavelengths and pulse characteristics can fragment particular ink particles.
◇  The essential principle
Target first. Technology second. Settings third. The machine name alone does not establish suitability.
✓  Laser is

items.text: A broad group of different devices
items.raw_text: • A broad group of different devices

items.text: Selected for a defined target
Dependent on wavelength and settings
Planned around skin and recovery

blocks.text: ◇  Laser is not

items.text: One procedure for every concern
The same as IPL
Automatically better at higher energy
A guarantee of perfect or identical results
items.raw_text: • A guarantee of perfect or identical results

blocks.text: Laser cannot responsibly promise poreless skin, complete scar removal, permanent removal of every hair or the same result for every person.
Technology should support your confidence, never replace careful judgment.
✦  Hair reduction
Progressive long-term reduction in the number, density or thickness of suitable hairs.
◇  Pigmentation
Selected pigmented lesions or uneven colour after the cause and pattern are considered.
○  Vessels and redness
Selected superficial vessels according to colour, depth, size and location.
◎  Texture, wrinkles and scars
Controlled thermal or ablative treatment that may support remodelling.
↗  Tattoos and micropigmentation
Fragmentation of selected pigments over a planned series.
⌁  Resurfacing
A more intensive renewal pathway with meaningful preparation and recovery.
Your skin deserves the option that fits it, not simply the strongest one.
Harmony Laser Platform

Harmony is a multi-technology platform. Its applications depend on the exact handpiece, wavelength and settings selected. According to the available configuration, it may be considered for selected pigmentary, vascular, texture, hair-reduction, scar or tattoo-related concerns.
LightSheer Duet

LightSheer Duet is a diode-laser system used for progressive long-term hair reduction. Treatment is adapted according to skin tone, hair colour and thickness, treatment area, recent sun exposure and previous response.
AcuPulse CO₂

AcuPulse is an ablative carbon-dioxide laser used for selected resurfacing treatments. Because it intentionally affects the skin barrier, it requires more preparation and recovery than a superficial facial procedure.
Hair grows in phases. Because only part of the hair is in a responsive stage at each appointment, treatment is usually planned as a series.
✦  Hair colour
Darker hair generally offers a clearer pigment target. Very light, grey, white and some red hair may respond poorly.
◇  Hair thickness
Coarser and finer hair may respond differently.
○  Skin tone
Melanin is also present in the skin, so device and settings must respect surrounding tissue.
◎  Recent tanning
Tanning changes energy absorption and may affect treatment timing.
↗  Treatment area
Facial and body hair do not always follow the same rhythm.
⌁  Hormonal influences
Some hair patterns may continue to develop and may require maintenance or investigation.
✓  What it may offer

items.text: Fewer suitable hairs
Slower regrowth
Finer remaining hair
Longer intervals between regrowth

blocks.text: ◇  What it cannot guarantee

items.text: Removal of every hair
The same number of sessions for everyone
items.raw_text: • The same number of sessions for everyone

items.text: No future hormonal hair growth
A strong response from hair without enough pigment
items.raw_text: • A strong response from hair without enough pigment

blocks.text: A gradual result can still be a meaningful and beautiful change.
Fractional does not mean superficial.
Fractional CO₂ laser treats microscopic areas while leaving surrounding skin untreated. This pattern supports healing, but the procedure still intentionally disrupts the barrier.

items.text: Selected acne scars and other carefully assessed scars;
Fine lines and wrinkles;
Uneven texture;
Selected signs of sun-related skin change.

blocks.text: Depth and density affect both the possible treatment response and the recovery burden. More intensity can mean more redness, swelling, peeling, sensitivity and risk.
Good preparation gives your skin the respect it deserves.

◇  Active irritation

○  Infection

◎  An unexplained lesion

Some conditions, healing patterns or medicines may affect treatment choice.
⌁  Limited recovery time

Waiting can be a positive choice when it protects your skin.

blocks.text: ◇  Possible temporary responses
Depending on the technology: redness, warmth, tenderness, swelling, darkening of treated pigment, dryness, crusting or peeling.

items.text: Cleanse gently according to the instructions received;
Use approved moisturising or repair care;
Protect the area from sun exposure;
Do not pick, scratch or remove crusts or flakes;
Pause retinoids, acids and exfoliants until cleared;
Modify heat, exercise, swimming or other procedures when instructed.

blocks.text: !  Possible risks

Laser can involve burns, blistering, prolonged redness or swelling, pigment changes, delayed healing, infection, cold-sore reactivation, acne-like eruptions, scarring or incomplete improvement. Risk varies with technology, settings, skin, technique and aftercare.
Questions I am often asked
Can laser remove pores?
No. Pores are normal anatomical structures. Certain resurfacing treatments may refine texture and their appearance.
Can laser erase a scar?
It may improve selected scar features, but it cannot restore injured skin exactly to its previous state.
Is laser hair removal permanent?
The most accurate term is long-term hair reduction. Maintenance may be needed.
Can the laser be chosen from a photograph?
A photograph can begin a conversation, but it cannot confirm cause, depth, suitability or settings.
The best plan leaves you informed, supported and still recognisably yourself.

blocks.text: Be heard, understand your options and choose without pressure.

I want aesthetic care to leave you feeling more informed and more comfortable in your own decisions. That means making room for what concerns you without turning every natural feature into something that needs treatment.
You deserve an honest conversation about what may help, what may not and what can reasonably be expected.
What you can expect

items.text: Time to explain the concern in your own words
A recommendation with a clear reason
Respect for your features, preferences and boundaries
Preparation and aftercare that make sense for the procedure

blocks.text: What I will not do

items.text: Create urgency around an elective decision
Recommend more simply because more is available
Promise a result that cannot be guaranteed
Proceed when timing, health or skin condition makes waiting safer

blocks.text: You deserve care that helps you feel more confident, while still feeling completely like yourself.

sections.label: THE CHANGE
sections.eyebrow: ○  03 · THE CHANGE I WANT TO CREATE
sections.heading: Listening Changes the Plan

blocks.text: Your concern
I first want to understand what you notice, how it affects you and why you are seeking guidance now.
Your context
Health history, medication, previous procedures, routine and upcoming commitments can all change what is sensible.
Your priorities
The degree of change you want and the recovery you can manage belong in the decision.
Your questions
Uncertainty is welcome. Questions help make consent more informed and the plan more useful.
Listening is not a courtesy added to treatment. It is clinical information that can change the recommendation.

items.title: Listen
items.text: Understand the concern, the hoped-for change and what would still feel natural to you.

items.title: Assess
items.text: Consider the area, your history, current condition, previous care and possible contraindications.

items.title: Explain
items.text: Describe suitable options, limits, preparation, recovery and alternatives in plain language.

items.title: Decide
items.text: Give you space to proceed, prepare, wait or decline without being rushed.

items.title: Review
items.text: Use your response to decide whether to continue, adjust, simplify or stop.

blocks.text: Confidence grows when you understand the choice and know that it remains yours.
Clinical judgment
Technology, treatment depth and settings need to match the intended target and the person receiving care.
Human attention
Comfort, privacy, fears and practical limitations deserve the same careful conversation.
Clear limitations
I will explain uncertainty and what a procedure cannot do, not only its possible benefits.
Appropriate referral
If a concern needs medical investigation or another professional’s assessment, that is the responsible next step.
You should never have to choose between feeling cared for and receiving an honest answer.
My aim is not to standardise faces, skin or bodies. I consider proportion, movement and the features that make you recognisable before discussing change.
Expression
Treatment planning should leave room for the way you naturally communicate and move.
Proportion
Another person’s photograph cannot define what will look or feel right for you.
Restraint
A smaller intervention, a slower plan or no procedure may be the most appropriate answer.
Confidence
Care should support how you feel, not create a new list of things to fear or correct.
Beauty feels most personal when your character is still present in the result.

items.title: Prepare
items.text: Receive instructions that reflect the specific procedure and your current skin or treatment area.

items.title: Check
items.text: Disclose relevant health changes, medication, sun exposure and recent procedures before treatment.

items.title: Recover
items.text: Understand expected temporary effects, practical restrictions and home care.

items.title: Contact
items.text: Know which changes should be reported and how to reach Franciele with a concern.

items.title: Follow up
items.text: Return for review when the procedure or your response calls for it.

blocks.text: Looking after yourself includes giving recovery the time and attention it deserves.

items.text: Speak about a sensitive concern without embarrassment.
Ask why an option is being suggested or ruled out.
Say when a result, recovery period or treatment idea does not feel right.
Take time to think after the consultation.
Change your mind before giving procedure-specific consent.

blocks.text: A calm clinic experience is not about silence. It is about having enough space to speak, understand and make a considered choice.
Your questions are part of your care, and you deserve answers you can genuinely understand.
Sometimes treatment waits
Active irritation, recent sun exposure, health changes or an unsuitable recovery window may make postponement appropriate.
Sometimes less is enough
A simpler routine or a smaller intervention can be more useful than adding procedures.
Sometimes another opinion matters
Medical symptoms, uncertain diagnoses or concerns outside aesthetic scope should be assessed by the appropriate professional.
Sometimes the plan changes
Progress, tolerance and priorities may lead Franciele to alter intervals, settings or the treatment route.
New technology is valuable when it improves judgment and care, not when it replaces them.
The highest standard is not doing everything possible; it is choosing what is responsible for you.
I want you to leave feeling listened to, respected and clear about what comes next. If treatment is appropriate, you should know why. If it is not, you should understand that decision too.
Heard
Your concern is understood in your own words.
Informed
The recommendation, limitations and alternatives are clear.
Comfortable
You can ask, pause or decline without awkwardness.
Supported
Preparation, aftercare and necessary review remain connected.
Caring for yourself is not about becoming someone else. It is about feeling worthy of your own attention.

◇  Early recovery

sections.label: NATURAL-LOOKING RESULTS
sections.heading: Improvement That Still Looks Like You

blocks.text: Natural-looking care is not one fixed aesthetic. It means the degree of change respects the person’s anatomy, expression, preferences and sense of self.
✦  Expression

◇  Proportion
The face or body is assessed as a whole rather than as isolated flaws.
○  Restraint

◎  Preference
A subtle change and a more visible change are not the same request.
↗  Identity
The objective is not to copy another person’s appearance.
⌁  Time
Gradual change may feel more appropriate than an immediate transformation.
A photograph is only an example; your own progress deserves its own measure.
✓  Care may help

Refine texture
Soften scar features
Support mild tightening

blocks.text: A routine should support your confidence, not make care feel impossible.
✦  Acne and congestion
Blackheads, whiteheads, inflammatory lesions, oil and post-acne marks do not all need the same approach.
◇  Pigmentation
Dark marks may have different causes, depths and triggers.
○  Sensitivity and redness
Burning, stinging, flushing or persistent redness may reflect irritation or another condition.
◎  Texture and pores
Pores are normal structures whose appearance can be influenced by oil, congestion, elasticity and light.
↗  Marks and scars
Flat colour changes and structural scars are different concerns.
⌁  Lines and photoageing
Ageing reflects time, genetics, movement, hormones and environmental exposure.
“Oily skin does not need moisturiser.”
Oil and water balance are not the same. Oily or acne-prone skin can still become irritated and uncomfortable.
“Blackheads are dirt.”
Blackheads are open comedones. Their dark appearance comes from follicular contents being exposed to air—not poor hygiene.
“Scrubbing clears acne faster.”
Aggressive scrubbing can increase irritation, colour change and discomfort.
“Pores can be permanently closed.”
Pores are normal anatomical openings. Their appearance may be refined, not removed.
“Tingling proves a product is working.”
Tingling can also signal irritation. Effective care does not need to burn.
“More peeling means a better treatment.”
Visible peeling is not a reliable measure of quality and can simply indicate more inflammation.
Comfort is valuable information, not something you need to push through.
Pigment can appear or deepen after sunlight, acne, irritation, injury, hormonal change, friction, medication or poorly timed procedures.
✦  Pattern
Is it isolated, scattered, symmetrical or widespread?
◇  Trigger
Did it follow acne, pregnancy, irritation, treatment or sun exposure?
○  Behaviour
Is it fading, stable, deepening or returning?
◎  Skin response
Does the skin inflame or mark easily?
↗  Maintenance
Can the necessary routine and protection be followed?
⌁  Medical review
A new, changing, bleeding or unexplained lesion should not be treated as a routine mark.
☼  Sun protection is a system
Broad-spectrum sunscreen, shade, clothing, sensible exposure and reapplication work together. Tinted protection may be discussed for selected pigmentary concerns.
Consistency can bring more confidence than chasing every new product.
Acne is a complex inflammatory condition involving the follicle, oil production, cell build-up and microbial activity. It is not evidence that the skin is dirty.

blocks.text: The principles you can expect to experience in Franciele’s care.
Franciele first asks what you have noticed, what you hope may change and how the concern affects you. You do not need the correct treatment name or clinical language.
You have time
Sensitive concerns can be explained in your own way, with room for questions and uncertainty.
Context is useful
Previous care, routine, health information and practical commitments help shape the advice.
Priorities matter
The result you want and the amount of change you feel comfortable with are discussed openly.
Feeling heard can be the first step towards feeling more at ease with yourself.
A procedure is considered because it may fit your concern and circumstances, not because it is new, popular or available.
Individual assessment
Skin characteristics, treatment history, health context and contraindications may affect the choice.
Right timing
Treatment may be delayed when the area needs preparation or recovery would be difficult to manage.
Another route
Franciele may suggest a different approach, observation or assessment by another professional.
The right choice is the one that respects you, not the one receiving the most attention.
You should understand what a recommendation is intended to address and what it cannot promise.
Before deciding
Franciele explains the purpose, alternatives, important limitations and likely treatment sequence.
Before treatment
Preparation, expected temporary reactions and practical restrictions are discussed.
After treatment
You receive relevant aftercare and know when a change should be reported.
Clear information gives you the confidence to choose at your own pace.
More treatment is not automatically better treatment. The plan should have a reason for each step.
Enough can be enough
Franciele may recommend a smaller intervention, fewer treatment areas or time to review.
Sequence matters
Combining procedures only makes sense when their timing and cumulative recovery are appropriate.
Natural movement matters
Facial planning considers expression and recognisable features, not a standardised ideal.
Confidence does not need excess; it often grows from choices that feel measured and true to you.
Your features, skin, routine and preferences are not interchangeable with anyone else’s.
Your starting point
Assessment is based on what is present today, rather than an edited image or another patient’s result.
Your idea of change
Subtle, gradual or more visible goals require different conversations and may have different limits.
Your life
Work, travel, events, sun exposure and available recovery time can affect scheduling.
The most beautiful improvement is one that leaves you feeling recognisably yourself.
Safety includes knowing when to adjust, postpone or advise against an elective procedure.

items.title: Screen
items.text: Review relevant health information, medication, sensitivities, recent procedures and current skin condition.

items.title: Prepare
items.text: Give procedure-specific instructions and allow time for the area to become ready when needed.

items.title: Treat
items.text: Use the selected technology or technique for a defined purpose within professional scope.

items.title: Support
items.text: Explain expected recovery, warning signs and the appropriate contact route.

blocks.text: Looking after yourself also means accepting when the safest answer is to wait.
Results, comfort, recovery and treatment duration differ between people. Franciele discusses that uncertainty directly.
No guaranteed result
Before-and-after images and testimonials are examples, not promises of an identical response.
Limits are part of consent
You should know where improvement may be modest, gradual, temporary or require maintenance.
Plans can change
Your actual response may support continuing, adjusting, simplifying or stopping.
An honest answer can protect your confidence far more than an impossible promise.
Respect means your comfort, privacy and boundaries remain part of the clinical decision.

items.text: Sensitive concerns are discussed discreetly.
Questions are answered without making you feel difficult.
You can ask for time, decline an option or change your mind.
Clinical photography and any wider use require the appropriate permission.
A consultation does not oblige you to have a procedure.

blocks.text: You deserve care that protects your dignity as carefully as it considers the result.
Consultation, preparation, treatment, aftercare and review work as one connected process.
Continuity
Instructions and follow-up reflect what was actually performed and how you respond.
Availability
You are told how to contact Franciele if recovery differs from what was explained.
Responsibility
Care may include postponement, referral or discontinuing a plan that no longer appears appropriate.
Reflection
Progress is reviewed against your own starting point, not someone else’s outcome.
You are worthy of care that remains attentive after the procedure is over.
